metadata.title: Build a Real-Time Verdict Module with Claude Code

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Olá, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?
props.segment: Build a Real-Time Verdict Module with

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes -- BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Fast enough on average is how safety-critical AI fails. The model passes every test -- sixty-five milliseconds mean latency, deadline is one hundred milliseconds. Then a GC pause fires during inference. One run takes a hundred and twenty-five milliseconds and the deadline is missed. The deadline you must never miss isn't the mean -- it's the worst case. WCET: worst-case execution time. A model that passes on mean latency can fail on WCET.
beats.visual_intent: title card: mean latency passes the deadline, WCET does not; the WCET tail extending past the deadline line

beats.narration_text: Ask for the experiment. In the terminal: claude -- write realtime.py, inputs deadline in milliseconds, deadline class, and an array of measured latencies. Compute WCET from the tail, not the mean. Classify soft, firm, or hard. Emit a verdict plus a recommended pattern if the model sits in a hard safety loop.

props.command:
claude "write realtime.py:
  inputs deadline_ms, deadline_class,
  measured_latencies[].
  Compute WCET (tail, not mean),
  classify soft/firm/hard,
  emit verdict + pattern."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. WCET is the ninety-ninth percentile of the latency distribution -- not the mean, not the maximum of a short run, but the tail. A soft deadline accepts occasional misses. A firm deadline treats any miss as a failure. A hard deadline requires the system to prove no miss is possible -- WCET must be below the deadline with margin.

props.code:
# realtime.py  --  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

def realtime_verdict(deadline_ms, deadline_class, latencies):
    mean_ms = np.mean(latencies)
    wcet_ms = np.percentile(latencies, 99)  # tail
    passed  = wcet_ms <= deadline_ms
    margin  = deadline_ms - wcet_ms
    verdict = {
        "mean_ms":    round(mean_ms, 1),
        "wcet_ms":    round(wcet_ms, 1),
        "deadline_ms": deadline_ms,
        "class":      deadline_class,
        "passed":     passed,
        "margin_ms":  round(margin, 1),
    }
    if deadline_class == 'hard' and not passed:
        verdict['pattern'] = 'anytime-algorithm or shadow-mode'
    return verdict

beats.role_note: OPEN vox slot -- Manim video (illustrative data)
beats.narration_text: Run it. The latency histogram builds bar by bar. Fifteen bins from thirty to one-fifty milliseconds. Most runs cluster around sixty-five milliseconds -- the bell peak. Three bins extend past the hundred millisecond deadline -- those bars are crimson. The deadline line appears. WCET: one hundred twenty-five milliseconds. Deadline missed.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: latency histogram, bins left to right, tail bins past 100ms deadline in CRIMSON, vertical deadline line, chip WCET: 125ms DEADLINE MISSED.

beats.narration_text: That is the baseline. Add jitter. GC pauses and DMA stalls push the tail out further. Iterate: claude -- add jitter from a GC and DMA stall and show the tail blow past the deadline.

props.command:
claude "add jitter from a GC / DMA stall
  and show the tail blow past the deadline"

beats.role_note: OPEN vox slot -- Manim video (illustrative data)
beats.narration_text: Re-run with GC jitter. New crimson bars appear at one hundred thirty to one hundred eighty milliseconds, well past the original tail. The mean is still sixty-five milliseconds -- unchanged. WCET is now one hundred eighty milliseconds. The deadline was one hundred. GC jitter alone doubles the worst-case. The average tells you nothing useful here.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: same histogram, additional CRIMSON bars extend to 180ms, chip GC JITTER TAIL EXTENDS, WCET: 180ms.

beats.narration_text: Real-time isn't fast -- it's bounded. The average latency tells you what normally happens. WCET tells you what the system guarantees under load. Optimizing the average while ignoring the tail is the classic safety failure. The GC pause doesn't care that you're running a neural network.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: two rows -- average latency: normal case; WCET: guarantee under load

beats.narration_text: Your move: collect a real latency distribution from your target under full production load -- not a synthetic benchmark, but the actual inference times during normal operation. Run realtime.py on them and check whether WCET fits inside your deadline class. The output beats in this video use illustrative distributions. Swap in your measured array to get the honest verdict.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) collect real latency distribution under load, 2) run realtime.py, 3) check WCET vs deadline class

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Real-time isn't fast -- it's bounded. The average latency tells you what normally happens. WCET tells you what the system guarantees under load. Optimizing the average while ignoring the tail is the classic safety failure. The GC pause doesn't care that you're running a neural network. Your move: collect a real latency distribution from your target under full production load -- not a synthetic benchmark, but the actual inference times during normal operation. beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
